title: The Lies Characters Tell: Utilizing Large Language Models to Normalize Adversarial Unicode Perturbations
contributions_summary: This paper introduces two novel large language model (LLM)-based methods—indirect and direct homoglyph normalization—that effectively unmask offensive content concealed by visually similar Unicode characters without relying on static mapping tables. Indirect normalization replaces non-Latin characters with delimiters and prompts LLMs to infer contextually appropriate replacements, while direct normalization instructs LLMs to directly substitute homoglyphs with their Latin equivalents, leveraging their internal linguistic knowledge; GPT-4o-mini’s direct method achieved state-of-the-art normalization quality with 0.96 cosine similarity to original text. Crucially, when applied as preprocessing, GPT-4o-mini’s direct normalization restored hate speech detection model performance to near-human-normalized levels in both zero-shot and cross-validation settings, significantly outperforming traditional tools like Unidecode and Unicode NFC/NFKD. The study demonstrates that LLMs can generalize to unseen homoglyphs, offering a dynamic, scalable defense against adversarial obfuscation in real-world social media text.
